narrative.summary: Kimono, ceramics, calligraphy supplies, ikebana, green tea, incense—old-school Japanese gift shop with wabi-sabi sensibility. 4.6 stars. Worth a focused stop when John is mapping the next design-conscious neighborhood run.

blocks.title: The Space
blocks.body: You'll find it on Harbord in the tangle of streets between the Annex and Kensington, a residential block that still holds onto a few independent shops. Inside, it's a small, carefully edited room—tatami-lined shelves, low wooden tables displaying lacquerware and ceramic tea bowls, the smell of sandalwood and hinoki. Owner Linda has arranged everything with ikebana-level restraint: silk kimonos hung on one wall, calligraphy brushes in bamboo holders, nothing fighting for your attention.

blocks.title: The Food
blocks.body: This isn't a restaurant—it's a gift shop focused on Japanese cultural objects and homeware. You'll find matcha whisks and ceremonial-grade green tea, proper miso bowls and chopsticks that feel balanced in your hand, incense from small makers in Kyoto. Prices reflect the quality: these aren't souvenir-shop versions but the real stuff, sourced with a wabi-sabi sensibility that values craft over abundance. Linda stocks what she'd use herself—bowls, textiles, tools for tea ceremony—and she'll talk you through the provenance if you ask.

blocks.title: Go When
blocks.body: Make it a destination stop on a weekend morning when you've got time to linger and talk with Linda, who treats every visit like a minor cultural exchange. Or fold it into a design-focused walk through the neighbourhood—it's the kind of place that recalibrates your eye before you hit the next vintage furniture shop or ceramics studio.

blocks.title: The Vibe
blocks.body:
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ "Stopped by Things Japanese on Harbord and had such a great experience. Linda, the owner, is incredibly kind—we ended up chatting for a while, and she shared so much about Japanese culture. You can tell she’s truly passionate and knowledgeable.

The shop isn’t big, but it’s full of a nice variety of authentic Japanese items—incense, kimonos, kitchenware like miso bowls, and more. Not packed with tons of stuff, but everything there feels carefully chosen and well-made. Prices aren’t cheap, but fair for the quality.

Overall, it felt more like a cultural experience than just shopping. Definitely worth a visit if you’re in the area. The only downside is the street’s under construction and parking can be a bit tricky—but it’s a lovely little spot worth checking out."

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ "Came here to find a matcha bowl! It was a very nice small and quaint store but had a lot of beautiful Japanese items ☺️ there was a small selection of matcha bowls but they were very beautiful and unique - handmade from Japan. I found the bowl of my dreams and very happy with it 😊"

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ "What a wonderful little store!

I had the pleasure of visiting Things Japanese, and it was a lovely experience.

As I shopped, I chatted with the owner about my trip to Japan in the spring. She was so thoughtful and informative about Japanese culture. She made me feel very welcome and open to ask questions about various items in the shop.

There are a wide variety of things to choose from, including stationary, stickers, notebooks and journals, incense and holders, ceramics, cookware and chopsticks, books, Japanese clothing, women's accessories, textiles, and much more.

The prices to me seem to be comparable to buying things directly from Japan online. You have to consider shipping and customs fees as part of the final price. Bringing anything to Canada from Japan is not cheap.

If you love Japan or are feeling nostalgic from your last visit, you will not leave this store empty handed!"
